text: This next case is a laparoscopic removal of a left ovary secondary to endometriosis. In this ultrasound picture you can see a left ovary with endometriomas. You can also see surgically endometriosis implants on the liver. as well as endometriosis implants in the pelvis. Here you can see the left ovary with multiple cysts which is what we had seen on ultrasound and this ovary is now going to be removed secondary to recurrent endometriosis. This particular patient she had undergone a hysterectomy in addition to a right ovary removal secondary to endometriosis and pelvic pain. At the time all the endometriosis that was visible was removed She had a peritoneal stripping as well as endometriosis excision and as you can see the nature of endometriosis can be chronic and recurrent. In her case she presented again with endometriosis of her ovary as well as endometriosis in other areas of her pelvis and liver which were not seen before.

The surgeon inspects the right ureter, visible running from the kidney to the bladder, and identifies an endometriosis implant in the right pelvis where the ovary was previously removed. Using a laparoscopic hook, the surgeon spreads the tissue and employs Maryland bipolar forceps for grasping and cauterizing. The surgeon ablates an endometriosis implant located at the back of the vagina with the hook.

The surgeon identifies areas of endometriosis at the left pelvic brim near the descending colon, with visible pulsating vascular structures to the left of the implants. The descending colon is situated to the right. The surgeon carefully uses energy to ablate the endometrial implants. The video also shows the archway of the urocycal ligament, which is suspending the vagina following a previous hysterectomy.

The surgeon grasps the left ovary and drains the endometrioma, a chocolate cyst, to reduce its size for removal through the umbilicus. The assistant uses a suction irrigator to remove the drained cyst contents. The ovary, decompressed from three to four chocolate cysts, is placed in an endo pouch for extraction through the belly button.

Following the removal of the ovary, the surgeon inspects the interior of the bladder, revealing an endometriosis implant. This implant is visually identified within the bladder, correlating with the patient's symptoms of urinary tract infection and bladder spasms.

in about ten percent of cases most cases after removal of the uterus in one of the ovaries allows for enough symptom relief that most people don't require another surgery thank you for watching this video and please share with anyone who may have endometriosis or pelvic pain because as you know knowledge is never wasted
